---
title: "Blog control center"
subtitle: "One file to write, plan, and publish everything from."
format: html
toc: true
---

<!-- ========================================================================
HOW THIS FILE WORKS
=========================================================================

You edit ONLY this file. Everything else is generated.

  1. Write posts inside `POST:START` / `POST:END` blocks (copy the TEMPLATE).
  2. Set a post's `status:` to `ready` when it's done (drafts are ignored).
  3. Run the generator:

         cd blog
         python3 generate.py            # writes the real files
         python3 generate.py --check    # dry run, writes nothing

  For every `ready`/`published` post this creates:
     • ../_posts/<file_date>-<slug>.md   -> your al-folio blog post
     • substack/<slug>.md                -> paste-ready Substack version

STRUCTURE OF THIS FILE
  • BLOG-CONFIG  — author + shared styling, applied to every post. Edit once.
  • STYLE GUIDE  — voice + the canonical section skeleton (for you and the LLM).
  • SUBSTACK PLAYBOOK — how each post is made more discoverable.
  • THEMES       — one H1 per high-level topic you write about. Each theme has
                   a brief (LLM context), a backlog of ideas, and its posts.
                   Add a new theme by copying an existing `# THEME:` section.

RULES
  • `slug` must be unique across the whole file.
  • Posts are free-form. If you use `## ` headings, they become the post's
    sidebar table of contents automatically (Resources/References excluded);
    if you don't, the post simply has no sidebar TOC. Override with a `toc:`
    list in a post's metadata if you want to set it by hand.
========================================================================= -->

<!-- BLOG-CONFIG:START -->
```yaml
# Applied to every generated blog post. Edit once here.
author:
  name: Veith Weilnhammer
  url: "https://veithweilnhammer.github.io"
  affiliation: UC Berkeley, Helen Wills Neuroscience Institute

# CSS injected into each al-folio post's front matter (kept identical to the
# existing posts so nothing about the site's look changes).
styles: |
  .fake-img {
    background: #bbb;
    border: 1px solid rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.1);
    box-shadow: 0 0px 4px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.1);
    margin-bottom: 12px;
  }
  .fake-img p {
    font-family: monospace;
    color: white;
    text-align: left;
    margin: 12px 0;
    text-align: center;
    font-size: 14px;
  }
```
<!-- BLOG-CONFIG:END -->

# Style guide & voice

Fill this in with your own preferences — it is context for you and for any
language model helping you draft. Suggested starting points:

- **Audience:** curious non-specialists + peers; explain jargon in one clause.
- **Voice:** clear, calm, personal.
- **Length:** aim for **~2,000 words** per post (roughly 1,800–2,200) — long
  enough for a real essay, safely under Substack's email-clipping limit.
- **Structure:** free-form. Write a normal post — prose, whatever headings (if
  any) fit the piece. Nothing is enforced. If you do use `## ` headings, they
  become the post's sidebar table of contents automatically (a `## Resources`
  or `## References` heading is left out of the TOC).

# Substack playbook (make posts more visible)

These are baked into every generated `substack/<slug>.md` header as a checklist.
Fill the per-post `substack:` block to drive them:

- **`subtitle`** — one line that doubles as the SEO/preview hook. This is the
  single biggest lever for opens and search.
- **`tldr`** — one sentence placed at the very top so skimmers stay.
- **`tags`** — 3–6 topic tags for Substack's discovery/recommendations.
- **Cross-linking** — the generator auto-appends a canonical link back to the
  matching blog post; add 1–2 inline links to related posts as you write.
- **CTA** — a subscribe/share line is auto-appended; keep it.
- Extra levers to apply by hand in Substack: a strong first image, publish at a
  consistent time, enable Recommendations, and cross-post to Notes.

<!-- ========================================================================
REUSABLE POST TEMPLATE  —  copy the whole block below into a theme, then edit.
Set `status: ready` when you want it generated. Leave as `draft` to ignore it.
========================================================================= -->

### 📝 TEMPLATE — copy me

<!-- POST:START -->
```yaml
slug: my-post-slug            # unique; becomes the filename + blog URL
title: "Working title of the post"
description: "One-line summary (blog meta + Substack fallback subtitle)."
date: 2026-01-01              # date shown in the blog post
file_date:                    # optional: date used in the filename (defaults to date)
tags: [tag-one, tag-two]
featured: false
status: draft                 # draft | ready | published  (only ready/published generate)
# toc: [Section, Another Section]   # optional: force a sidebar TOC (else auto from ## headings, or none)
substack:
  subtitle: "Hook / SEO subtitle shown in previews and email."
  tldr: "One-sentence takeaway shown at the very top on Substack."
  tags: [neuroscience, psychiatry]
```

Write the post freely below — plain prose, with or without `## ` headings.

<!-- POST:END -->

<!-- ========================================================================
THEMES  —  one H1 per high-level topic. Duplicate a whole `# THEME:` block to
add a new area. Posts can live under whichever theme fits; slugs stay unique.
========================================================================= -->

# THEME: The Moving Mind — reading mental life from movement

**Brief (LLM context).** This series treats **movement as the visible surface of
mental life**. Its throughline: long before psychiatry measured molecules or
parsed words, it read the mind from motion — Darwin's trembling lip, the poker
player's tell, the slowed gait of depression, the flat gaze of schizophrenia.
The series traces that idea across history, philosophy, and neuroscience and
makes one argument in stages: that action and perception are a single
inferential loop (predictive coding / active inference); that psychiatry's
reliance on self-report leaves most of the mind unmeasured; and that new tools —
digital phenotyping, human–computer interaction — could recover a science of
*movement and meaning*, if we resist mistaking data for empathy.

**Voice.** Essayistic and historically grounded, one foot in the humanities
(Darwin, James, Merleau-Ponty, Goffman) and one in computational neuroscience
(Wolpert, Friston). Each piece opens with a concrete scene — a tell, a trembling
hand, a waiting-room posture — before turning to the argument. Audience: curious
general readers and researchers alike. Connects directly to my own work on
inference in perception and on decoding mental states from digital behavior
(MAILA).

**Recurring motifs** (weave through every post, don't state them mechanically):

- Movement as **symptom** (measurable, mute) vs movement as **expression**
  (meaningful, relational) — every post lives in this tension.
- **Inference under uncertainty** — the clinician, the poker player, and the
  brain are all Bayesian readers of partial evidence.
- The inherited **mind–body dualism** science claims to reject but reproduces in
  practice (reaction times as output, voxels as cause).
- **Attunement / synchrony** — the coordination of movement and meaning that
  makes social life possible, and what its breakdown reveals.
- **Leakage vs concealment** — the body as both instrument of expression and
  mask (Goffman's stage).
- **Data ≠ empathy** — finer instruments, thinner phenomena; measurement must
  coexist with interpretation.

## The arc

A ~14-part series in four movements. Order is a spine, not a straitjacket.

**Part I — Foundations: the old intuition**

| # | Working title | Angle | Anchors |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | The Oldest Form of Mind-Reading | Movement as a window into inner life, from facial expression to everyday gesture; why science lost the thread. | Darwin (1872); James (1884); Spinoza |
| 2 | The Tell | Leakage and inference under uncertainty — the mind breaking into public space, from poker to the clinic. | Ekman; Bernstein; Goffman (1959); Polanyi (tacit knowledge) |
| 3 | When Machines Learned to See Emotion | The dream of quantifying affect through motion — what affective computing got right and wrong. | Ekman & Friesen (FACS); Picard (1997) |
| 4 | The Body in the Machine | A philosophical turn: perception and action as one continuous loop; thinking as an embodied act. | Merleau-Ponty (1945); embodied cognition |

**Part II — Mechanism: the brain that moves to know**

| # | Working title | Angle | Anchors |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | Motor Control as Inference | The brain as a predictive engine; every movement, even a blink, as probabilistic inference. | Wolpert & Kawato (1998); Shadmehr & Krakauer (2008); Friston (2010) |
| 6 | Thinking Is a Motor Act | How movement doesn't just reflect cognition but partly constitutes it. | embodied cognition; active inference |
| 7 | The Non-Verbal Turn in Psychiatry | How psychiatry recognized that motion can diagnose before words do — and then forgot. | Kraepelin; Bleuler (1911, affective flattening); Andreasen (1979, psychomotor slowing) |
| 8 | When Attunement Breaks | Catatonia, Parkinson's, schizophrenia, autism — the loss of coordination between movement and meaning. | phenomenology of synchrony; catatonia literature |

**Part III — Measurement: the present crisis**

| # | Working title | Angle | Anchors |
|---|---|---|---|
| 9 | The Limits of Self-Report | Why we still measure mind from what people say, not what they do; how rating scales flatten. | Insel et al. (2010, RDoC) |
| 10 | The Rise of Digital Phenotyping | What digital behavior promised — and why it hasn't yet delivered individual understanding. | Onnela & Rauch (2016) |
| 11 | The Mind in the Cursor | Human–computer interaction as a non-verbal channel; my own work decoding mental states from digital behavior. | MAILA (Weilnhammer et al.); digital phenotyping |
| 12 | Data Without Theory | The recurring temptation to measure movement without meaning — from phrenology to the polygraph to micro-expression training. | history of pseudo-quantification |

**Part IV — Ethics & future: movement and meaning**

| # | Working title | Angle | Anchors |
|---|---|---|---|
| 13 | The Ethics of Invisible Measurement | Turning ordinary interaction into data — the line between empathy and surveillance. | Mittelstadt et al. (2016) |
| 14 | Toward a New Language of Behavior | Imagining a shared, interpretable vocabulary of movement; recovery as the return of synchrony. | Saxe (theory of mind); synthesis |

**Substack framing.** Publish as a serialized essay series ("The Moving Mind").
Target **~2,000 words per post** (1,800–2,200). Each post's `subtitle` carries
the hook and doubles as SEO; open on a concrete scene; link forward to the next
entry and back to the previous one; anchor the first and last posts with a
subscribe CTA. Drafts for #1 and #2 already exist in `blog/idea.md`.

## Articles

<!-- Each article is scaffolded as a draft POST block. Flip `status:` to `ready`
and write the body when you want it generated. Angles + anchors live in the
comment at the top of each body. Slugs are set; dates are added when ready. -->

### 1 · The Oldest Form of Mind-Reading

<!-- POST:START -->
```yaml
slug: oldest-form-of-mind-reading
title: "The Oldest Form of Mind-Reading"
description: "Why science still struggles to understand the mind through movement."
date: 2026-07-05
tags: [movement, mind, psychiatry, history, perception]
featured: false
status: ready
substack:
  subtitle: "Long before we measured the brain, we read the mind from motion. Then we forgot how."
  tldr: "Movement has always been a window into mental life — and psychiatry's turn to words and voxels left most of the mind unmeasured."
  tags: [neuroscience, psychiatry, philosophy of mind, history of science]
```

## A mouth that cannot speak

Before the first sentence in a clinic, there is already evidence. Someone pauses at the door a fraction too long. Their shoulders stay lifted after the coat comes off. The hand offered in greeting is warm, cold, hesitant, overfirm. A chair is chosen, crossed toward, avoided. The person says, eventually, that they are fine; but the body has been speaking in tense, timing, and trajectory for several minutes.

This is not mind-reading in the supernatural sense. It is older and more ordinary than that. Human beings are animals who infer one another through movement. We notice how a face turns away, how a voice thins, how a step speeds up when a name is called. Long before we had diagnostic manuals, brain scanners, or rating scales, we had this capacity: to see another body in motion and sense that something inward was unfolding there.

The claim of this series is simple, though its consequences are not: movement is the visible surface of mental life. Thought does not merely produce action after the fact. Feeling does not sit privately inside the skull and then send instructions outward. Mind is continuously appearing in posture, gesture, gaze, breath, rhythm, and hesitation. To watch movement carefully is not to inspect a crude output of cognition. It is to stand at one of the places where cognition becomes public.

## Darwin's trembling lip

Charles Darwin gave this intuition its great nineteenth-century form. In *The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals*, published in 1872, he treated expression not as decoration added to emotion, but as part of emotion's natural history. The face, the hand, the voice, the skin, the viscera: all carried traces of actions that had once been useful. He called his first principle “the principle of serviceable associated Habits.” Movements that helped an animal survive, escape, attack, soothe, reject, or attend could persist as expressive remnants, reappearing when a related state of mind returned.

Darwin's fear is not an idea locked behind the eyes. It trembles. In Chapter XII he writes: “One of the best-marked symptoms is the trembling of all the muscles of the body; and this is often first seen in the lips. From this cause, and from the dryness of the mouth, the voice becomes husky or indistinct, or may altogether fail.” The passage is powerful because nothing in it is theatrical. Fear is not announced; it interferes. It enters the lips, the mouth, the muscles of phonation. It makes speech unreliable.

Darwin was not the first to think this way. Hippocratic medicine built its authority on reading bodily signs — posture, gait, agitation, the look of the eyes — as indices of internal disorder. Artists and rhetoricians studied gesture because they knew that persuasion and feeling lived partly in the body. Spinoza, writing in the seventeenth century, refused to split mind and body into separate kingdoms: joy and sadness were, simultaneously and indivisibly, increases or decreases in the body's power of acting. William James would later sharpen the provocation with the phrase “afraid because we tremble.”

These thinkers differed in method, metaphysics, and evidence. But they shared a thread that modern science has often misplaced: inner life is not simply hidden. It shows itself, imperfectly but constantly, in movement.

## How the thread was lost

The loss did not happen because scientists stopped caring about behavior. In one sense, the twentieth century became obsessed with it. Behaviorism made movement the official subject of psychology. But it did so by bracketing the very thing that made movement psychologically interesting. A response could be counted, timed, reinforced, extinguished. The mind behind it was declared a black box.

Psychoanalysis moved in the opposite direction. It restored depth, conflict, fantasy, memory, and meaning; but it often turned the body into a text to be interpreted rather than an organism moving in real time. A twitch became a symbol, a posture a clue, a silence a displacement. Then neuroscience arrived with new authority and new instruments. The decisive reality seemed to be inside the brain: circuits, transmitters, voxels, networks. The rest of the body remained present, of course, but often as downstream effect.

Each tradition preserved part of the truth and discarded another. Behaviorism kept movement but emptied it of inner life. Psychoanalysis kept inner life but risked floating away from measurement. Neuroscience brought mechanism but often narrowed the stage to the skull. Somewhere in this history, psychology and psychiatry lost confidence in the idea that thinking is a motor act.

By that I do not mean that every thought is a visible gesture, or that mental life can be reduced to muscle. I mean something quieter and more radical: cognition is organized for action. Perception prepares movement; movement tests perception. Even stillness has a motor structure. A person who does not speak is not therefore inert. Waiting, withholding, freezing, bracing, turning away — these are actions too.

## The clinical eye

Psychiatry has never truly escaped this knowledge. The diagnostic interview may be built around speech, but the clinician is always watching. Depression can enter the room as slowed initiation, lowered volume, delayed response, diminished gesture. Mania may arrive as pressured speech, restless pacing, accelerated shifts of attention. Anxiety changes breath and muscle tone. Catatonia makes the collapse of voluntary movement impossible to ignore.

These observations are not merely poetic. Psychomotor slowing is central to melancholic depression and has been treated as a strong marker of illness subtype and course, particularly in the work of Parker and Hadzi-Pavlovic and in later reviews of psychomotor retardation. In schizophrenia, affective flattening was defined through careful clinical observation of reduced expressivity. Eye movement abnormalities, especially in smooth pursuit and saccadic control, are found in schizophrenia and in some individuals at clinical high risk, including people who later develop psychosis. The body often knows how to enter the record before language does.

And yet these signs sit uneasily in modern psychiatry. They are visible, but hard to standardize. They are measurable, but not exhausted by measurement. A slow reply may reflect depression, medication, fatigue, deliberation, suspicion, or cultural style. Averted gaze may mean shame, concentration, fear, respect, or nothing stable at all. Movement is informative because it is embodied and contextual; those are also the reasons it resists easy coding.

This creates a familiar oscillation. One approach treats movement as symptom: a motor parameter to be extracted, scored, and correlated. Another treats it as expression: meaningful, relational, dependent on the situation and the observer. The first promises rigor but risks thinning the phenomenon. The second preserves meaning but risks becoming private intuition. The science we need has to hold both.

## Thinking is a motor act

A better account begins from the loop. We do not passively receive a world and then decide how to move in it. We move in order to perceive, and perceive in order to move. The eyes sample; the head turns; the hand reaches; posture prepares possible futures. Modern theories of motor control and inference give this old intuition new form. Wolpert and Kawato's work on paired forward and inverse models, and Friston's free-energy framework, point toward a brain that predicts the sensory consequences of action and updates itself through error. That argument belongs to a later essay, but its implication matters here: action and perception are not separate chapters.

If thinking is a motor act, then movement is not just evidence of a prior mental state. It is one of the ways mental states are made, stabilized, and shared. On this view a gesture is not only a report on thought but part of how thought takes shape, and expression and feeling arise together — much as Darwin and Spinoza each in their way suggested — rather than one simply causing the other. Social life depends on these loops multiplying between people: my timing changes yours; your hesitation changes my prediction; our conversation becomes a choreography of inference under uncertainty.

This is why the oldest form of mind-reading is also the most fragile. We can read movement only probabilistically. There is no dictionary in which a tremor means fear, a smile means joy, a pause means sadness. Darwin knew this better than many of his descendants. His observations were comparative, patient, naturalistic. He did not offer a simple code; he traced the histories by which actions become expressions.

Our instruments are now far more powerful. Cameras can track the face in hundreds of points. Phones can record gait, sleep, speech, and social rhythm. Eye trackers can measure saccades too small for ordinary perception. These tools may help psychiatry recover what it misplaced, but only if they are guided by better questions. Data are not empathy. A feature vector is not an understanding. To measure movement without asking how it participates in meaning is simply to rebuild behaviorism with better sensors.

The danger is not measurement itself. Psychiatry needs measurement, and patients deserve more than impressions preserved in a clinician's private confidence. The danger is pretending that precision removes interpretation. A sensor can tell us that someone left the house less often this week, spoke more slowly, or slept at irregular hours. It cannot, by itself, know whether that pattern means grief, safety, exhaustion, protest, relapse, or recovery. The humane science of movement will have to be both statistical and situated: able to count without forgetting who is being counted, and able to interpret without claiming possession of another person's interior life.

The promise is not a machine that finally sees through people. It is a science that learns to describe what clinicians, artists, parents, lovers, and friends have always depended on: that mental life has a surface; that the surface moves; that another person is never fully transparent and never fully hidden.

This series begins there. Not with the fantasy of decoding the body once and for all, but with the more difficult task of following movement as it crosses biology, feeling, prediction, culture, illness, and care. The next question is what happens when movement betrays us — when a private state leaks into public view despite our best efforts to conceal it. That is the subject of the next essay, [The Tell](/blog/the-tell/). Subscribe or follow along if you want to read the series as it unfolds.

## References and further reading

- Darwin, Charles. *The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals*. London: John Murray, 1872.  
  Public domain text: https://darwin-online.org.uk/content/frameset?itemID=F1142&viewtype=text

- James, William. "What Is an Emotion?" *Mind* 9, no. 34 (1884): 188–205.  
  https://archive.org/details/mindos9birm/page/188/mode/2up

- Spinoza, Baruch. *Ethics*, Part III, Proposition 11 and Definitions of the Affects. 1677.  
  Public domain text (Elwes trans.): https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/3800

- Parker, Gordon, and Dusan Hadzi-Pavlovic, eds. *Melancholia: A Disorder of Movement and Mood*.  
  Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1996.

- Bennabi, Djamila, Pierre Vandel, Emmanuel Papaxanthis-Papaxanthis, Thierry Pozzo, and Emmanuel Haffen. "Psychomotor Retardation in Depression: A Systematic Review of Diagnostic, Pathophysiologic, and Therapeutic Implications." *BioMed Research International* 2013 (2013): 158746. https://doi.org/10.1155/2013/158746

- Andreasen, Nancy C. "Negative Symptoms in Schizophrenia: Definition and Reliability." *Archives of General Psychiatry* 39, no. 7 (1982): 784–788. https://doi.org/10.1001/archpsyc.1982.04290070020005

- Calkins, Monica E., William G. Iacono, and Denise S. Ones. "Eye Movement Dysfunction in Schizophrenia: A Meta-Analysis." *Schizophrenia Bulletin* 34, no. 4 (2008): 741–756. https://doi.org/10.1093/schbul/sbn046

- Wolpert, Daniel M., and Mitsuo Kawato. "Multiple Paired Forward and Inverse Models for Motor Control." *Neural Networks* 11, no. 7–8 (1998): 1317–1329. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0893-6080(98)00066-5

- Friston, Karl. "The Free-Energy Principle: A Unified Brain Theory?" *Nature Reviews Neuroscience* 11, no. 2 (2010): 127–138. https://doi.org/10.1038/nrn2787

- Walther, Sebastian, and Werner Strik. "Motor Symptoms and Schizophrenia." *Neuropsychobiology* 66, no. 2 (2012): 77–92. https://doi.org/10.1159/000339456

<!-- POST:END -->

### 2 · The Tell

<!-- POST:START -->
```yaml
slug: the-tell
title: "The Tell"
description: "The smallest possible mistake — how a private state breaks into public space."
tags: [movement, mind, inference, psychiatry, emotion]
featured: false
status: draft
substack:
  subtitle: "From the poker table to the clinic: why a trembling hand is inference, not a code."
  tldr: "A 'tell' is the visible footprint of a nervous system re-calibrating in real time — and reading it is an act of inference under uncertainty."
  tags: [neuroscience, psychiatry, decision making, emotion]
```
<!-- ANGLE: leakage and inference under uncertainty; the double life of movement (mechanical vs meaningful); the clinician as reader of tells; the ethics of reading. ANCHORS: Ekman; Bernstein 1967; Wolpert & Kawato; Goffman 1959; Polanyi. DRAFT MATERIAL: blog/idea.md ("The Tell"). -->

<!-- POST:END -->

### 3 · When Machines Learned to See Emotion

<!-- POST:START -->
```yaml
slug: machines-learned-to-see-emotion
title: "When Machines Learned to See Emotion"
description: "The dream of quantifying affect through motion — and what it got wrong."
tags: [movement, emotion, affective computing, measurement]
featured: false
status: draft
substack:
  subtitle: "We taught computers to read faces. What did we lose in making emotion legible?"
  tldr: "Affective computing promised to quantify feeling from movement; its successes and failures reveal the gap between signal and meaning."
  tags: [AI, affective computing, emotion, neuroscience]
```
<!-- ANGLE: the ambition to make affect machine-readable; FACS as muscular geometry of emotion; where quantification succeeds and where it strips context. ANCHORS: Ekman & Friesen (FACS); Picard 1997 (Affective Computing). -->

<!-- POST:END -->

### 4 · The Body in the Machine

<!-- POST:START -->
```yaml
slug: body-in-the-machine
title: "The Body in the Machine"
description: "Perception and action as one continuous loop."
tags: [movement, embodiment, philosophy, perception]
featured: false
status: draft
substack:
  subtitle: "We do not tremble because we are afraid; we are afraid because we tremble."
  tldr: "Phenomenology and embodied cognition recover an old idea: the mind is not hidden behind movement but enacted through it."
  tags: [philosophy of mind, embodied cognition, neuroscience]
```
<!-- ANGLE: the action-perception loop; embodiment against dualism; thinking as embodied enactment. ANCHORS: Merleau-Ponty 1945; James; embodied cognition. -->

<!-- POST:END -->

### 5 · Motor Control as Inference

<!-- POST:START -->
```yaml
slug: motor-control-as-inference
title: "Motor Control as Inference"
description: "The brain as a predictive engine — what movement reveals about internal models."
tags: [movement, predictive-coding, motor-control, inference, neuroscience]
featured: false
status: draft
substack:
  subtitle: "Every movement, even a blink, is a wager the brain makes about the world."
  tldr: "Internal models and active inference recast movement as probabilistic prediction — the same machinery that builds perception."
  tags: [computational neuroscience, predictive coding, motor control]
```
<!-- ANGLE: internal models; the brain predicting sensory consequences of its own actions; active inference as a unifying frame; why movements expose internal models. ANCHORS: Wolpert & Kawato 1998; Shadmehr & Krakauer 2008; Friston 2010. -->

<!-- POST:END -->

### 6 · Thinking Is a Motor Act

<!-- POST:START -->
```yaml
slug: thinking-as-a-motor-act
title: "Thinking Is a Motor Act"
description: "How movement doesn't just reflect cognition but partly constitutes it."
tags: [movement, embodied-cognition, consciousness, neuroscience]
featured: false
status: draft
substack:
  subtitle: "What if the mind becomes aware of itself by moving?"
  tldr: "If action and perception share one inferential process, then movement is not the output of thought but part of how thought happens."
  tags: [embodied cognition, consciousness, neuroscience]
```
<!-- ANGLE: movement as constitutive of cognition and self-awareness; consciousness as continuous calibration of movement and sensation. ANCHORS: embodied cognition; active inference. -->

<!-- POST:END -->

### 7 · The Non-Verbal Turn in Psychiatry

<!-- POST:START -->
```yaml
slug: non-verbal-turn-in-psychiatry
title: "The Non-Verbal Turn in Psychiatry"
description: "How psychiatry learned that motion can diagnose before words — and then forgot."
tags: [movement, psychiatry, diagnosis, history]
featured: false
status: draft
substack:
  subtitle: "Psychomotor slowing predicts depression better than mood ratings. Why don't we watch?"
  tldr: "Some of the most reliable signals of mental illness are motoric — yet modern psychiatry records words, not gestures."
  tags: [psychiatry, mental health, diagnosis, neuroscience]
```
<!-- ANGLE: the clinical eye as an observational science of tells; the drift from observation to molecule and voxel; motoric signs (catatonia, mania, flattening) that rarely enter formal models. ANCHORS: Kraepelin; Bleuler 1911; Andreasen 1979. -->

<!-- POST:END -->

### 8 · When Attunement Breaks

<!-- POST:START -->
```yaml
slug: when-attunement-breaks
title: "When Attunement Breaks"
description: "What disorders of movement reveal about the coordination of motion and meaning."
tags: [movement, psychiatry, schizophrenia, catatonia, attunement]
featured: false
status: draft
substack:
  subtitle: "When a person's movements fall out of sync with the world, communication becomes fragile."
  tldr: "Catatonia, Parkinson's, schizophrenia and autism expose attunement — the glue of social life — by removing it."
  tags: [psychiatry, schizophrenia, neurology, social cognition]
```
<!-- ANGLE: the thin boundary between intention and motion; attunement as the basis of mutual prediction; what its failure reveals. ANCHORS: catatonia literature; phenomenology of synchrony; Parkinson's/Huntington's. -->

<!-- POST:END -->

### 9 · The Limits of Self-Report

<!-- POST:START -->
```yaml
slug: limits-of-self-report
title: "The Limits of Self-Report"
description: "Why we still measure the mind from what people say, not what they do."
tags: [movement, psychiatry, measurement, self-report]
featured: false
status: draft
substack:
  subtitle: "Rating scales turn a rich human encounter into a number between zero and four."
  tldr: "Self-report remains psychiatry's gold standard even though much of mental life is enacted, not spoken."
  tags: [psychiatry, measurement, mental health, RDoC]
```
<!-- ANGLE: the diagnostic interview as a conversation that mostly records words; the abstraction of rating scales; the search for stable biomarkers. ANCHORS: Insel et al. 2010 (RDoC). -->

<!-- POST:END -->

### 10 · The Rise of Digital Phenotyping

<!-- POST:START -->
```yaml
slug: rise-of-digital-phenotyping
title: "The Rise of Digital Phenotyping"
description: "What digital behavior promised — and why it hasn't yet delivered."
tags: [movement, digital-phenotyping, mental-health, measurement]
featured: false
status: draft
substack:
  subtitle: "Our phones record everything. Why do they still understand so little?"
  tldr: "Digital phenotyping promised scalable insight into the mind; delivering individual understanding has proven harder than group averages."
  tags: [digital phenotyping, mental health, data science, psychiatry]
```
<!-- ANGLE: the promise of passive behavioral data; the gap between population signal and individual understanding. ANCHORS: Onnela & Rauch 2016. -->

<!-- POST:END -->

### 11 · The Mind in the Cursor

<!-- POST:START -->
```yaml
slug: hci-as-behavior
title: "The Mind in the Cursor"
description: "Human–computer interaction as a non-verbal channel for reading mental state."
tags: [movement, digital-phenotyping, HCI, mental-health, machine-learning]
featured: false
status: draft
substack:
  subtitle: "The way you move a cursor carries information about your mind that words don't."
  tldr: "Cursor and touchscreen movement form a scalable, non-verbal signal of mental state — the same old intuition, made measurable."
  tags: [machine learning, digital phenotyping, mental health, HCI]
```
<!-- ANGLE: HCI as the modern tell; my own work (MAILA) decoding mental states from cursor/touch; movement as a scalable non-verbal modality. ANCHORS: MAILA (Weilnhammer et al.); links to the MAILA post. -->

<!-- POST:END -->

### 12 · Data Without Theory

<!-- POST:START -->
```yaml
slug: data-without-theory
title: "Data Without Theory"
description: "The recurring temptation to measure movement without meaning."
tags: [movement, measurement, epistemology, history]
featured: false
status: draft
substack:
  subtitle: "Phrenology, the polygraph, micro-expression training: each promised a shortcut to truth."
  tldr: "Without theory, ever-finer measurement of movement repeats an old mistake — treating the body as a code rather than an expression."
  tags: [history of science, measurement, epistemology, neuroscience]
```
<!-- ANGLE: cautionary history of pseudo-quantification; why data without theory recreates the symptom/expression error; measurement must coexist with interpretation. ANCHORS: phrenology; polygraph; micro-expression training. -->

<!-- POST:END -->

### 13 · The Ethics of Invisible Measurement

<!-- POST:START -->
```yaml
slug: ethics-of-invisible-measurement
title: "The Ethics of Invisible Measurement"
description: "Turning ordinary interaction into data — the line between help and surveillance."
tags: [movement, ethics, privacy, mental-health, digital-phenotyping]
featured: false
status: draft
substack:
  subtitle: "The power to read a tell comes with the responsibility to interpret it gently."
  tldr: "As our ability to measure movement outpaces our ability to interpret it, empathy and surveillance start to blur."
  tags: [ethics, privacy, AI, mental health]
```
<!-- ANGLE: reading a tell as crossing a boundary; empathy vs surveillance; visibility is not understanding; clinician and researcher responsibilities. ANCHORS: Mittelstadt et al. 2016. -->

<!-- POST:END -->

### 14 · Toward a New Language of Behavior

<!-- POST:START -->
```yaml
slug: new-language-of-behavior
title: "Toward a New Language of Behavior"
description: "Imagining a shared, interpretable vocabulary of movement."
tags: [movement, future, social-cognition, psychiatry, synthesis]
featured: false
status: draft
substack:
  subtitle: "What if non-verbal signals became a shared language we could actually read?"
  tldr: "The next science of the mind may depend less on new instruments than on new questions about movement, meaning, and recovery."
  tags: [neuroscience, psychiatry, future of medicine, social cognition]
```
<!-- ANGLE: series synthesis; a future vocabulary of movement; recovery as the return of synchrony; the questions that matter more than the instruments. ANCHORS: Saxe (theory of mind); synthesis of the series. -->

<!-- POST:END -->


# THEME: Topic B — rename me

**Brief (LLM context):** describe the second high-level topic here.

**Backlog / ideas:**

- [ ] Idea one —
- [ ] Idea two —

<!-- Posts for this theme go here. -->
